questions.prompt: You are building a welcome screen for a Publica Academy app. Declare const variables for: studentName (any Nigerian name), city (any Nigerian city), courseName = "JavaScript Fundamentals", moduleNumber = 3. Then use template literals to log all three of the following: (a) a personalised greeting: "Welcome to [courseName], [studentName]!"; (b) a location message: "You are learning from [city]."; (c) a progress message using an expression inside ${}: "You are on Module [moduleNumber] of 18. That is [round the percentage to a whole number]% complete." — calculate the percentage inside the ${}.

criteria.description: All three template literals produce the correct output; the percentage in (c) is calculated correctly inside ${} as Math.round(moduleNumber / 18 * 100) or equivalent; all variable values appear in the right positions
All three strings use backtick template literals with ${} for variable embedding — no string concatenation with +; the percentage expression is evaluated inside ${} without an intermediate variable
All four variables declared with const; camelCase naming; no var; no syntax errors

questions.prompt: You receive a Nigerian user's registration data from a form. The raw values are: rawEmail = "  BAYO@GMAIL.COM  ", rawPhone = "090-876-543-210", rawCity = "  port harcourt  ". Write script.js that: (a) cleans rawEmail using trim() and toLowerCase() and stores it in a const; (b) cleans rawPhone by removing all dashes using replaceAll() and stores it in a const; (c) cleans rawCity using trim() and then capitalises it — store the trimmed version (you do not need to capitalise each word, just store the trimmed version); (d) logs all three cleaned values with descriptive labels using template literals.

criteria.description: cleanEmail = "bayo@gmail.com" (trimmed and lowercased); cleanPhone = "090876543210" (all dashes removed); cleanCity = "port harcourt" (trimmed); all three logged with labels

trim().toLowerCase() chained for the email; replaceAll("-", "") used for the phone (not replace()); trim() used for the city; methods are chained where appropriate rather than stored in intermediate variables
Cleaned results stored in descriptively-named consts; template literals used for logging; no syntax errors; no var

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between template literals (backticks) and regular string quotes (single or double) in JavaScript. What are the two main advantages of template literals? Give a specific example showing a case where template literals are clearly the better choice.

criteria.description: Correctly identifies that template literals use backticks and support both variable embedding with ${} and multi-line strings without \n; correctly states that single/double quotes cannot do either of these things natively

Example shows a meaningful string that contains a variable — e.g. a greeting with a name — first written with concatenation (showing the messiness) and then with a template literal (showing the clarity)

questions.prompt: Write a Nigerian phone number validator. Declare const phone = "08012345678". Then log the result of all four checks with descriptive labels: (a) whether the phone number starts with "080", "081", "090", or "091" — check startsWith for each and combine with || to log a single boolean; (b) whether the phone number contains a dash (it should not — use includes("-") and ! to log whether it is dash-free); (c) whether the phone number is exactly 11 characters long using .length; (d) the network prefix using slice(0, 3).

criteria.description: All four results are correct: (a) true — startsWith("080") is true so the || chain is true; (b) true — no dash, so !includes("-") is true; (c) true — "08012345678".length === 11; (d) "080"
startsWith used for (a) with || combining at least two prefix checks; ! used to negate includes("-") for (b); .length property (not .length()) used for (c); slice(0, 3) used for (d)
All logs have descriptive labels; template literals or descriptive console.log calls used; no var; no syntax errors

questions.prompt: Declare const product = "Ankara Midi Dress — Size M — Colour: Red". Then write expressions (not stored in variables — directly inside console.log) for all four of the following: (a) log whether the product description includes the word "Dress"; (b) log the position of the word "Size" using indexOf; (c) log the last 5 characters of the string using slice with a negative index; (d) log an array of the three parts split by " — " (space-dash-dash-space) using split.

criteria.description: All four results correct: (a) true; (b) the index where "Size" starts (18); (c) " Red" — last 5 chars (space + Red + nothing, adjust: last 5 = "d Red" or "R Red" — exact answer depends on string length, marked correct if slice(-5) is used correctly); (d) array of three strings: ["Ankara Midi Dress", "Size M", "Colour: Red"]
includes() used for (a); indexOf() used for (b); slice() with a negative index used for (c); split(" — ") used for (d) — separator must include the spaces
All four expressions written directly inside console.log (no intermediate variables); descriptive labels on each log; no syntax errors

questions.prompt: Explain what `.indexOf()` returns and how it differs from `.includes()`. When would you choose indexOf over includes? What does a return value of -1 from indexOf mean, and how would you write a condition that checks whether something was NOT found?

criteria.description: Correctly explains that includes() returns a boolean (true/false) while indexOf() returns the numeric position of the first match or -1 if not found; correctly states that -1 means not found; correctly shows the check value === -1 for the not-found condition

Gives a concrete use case for preferring indexOf — e.g. needing to know where the @ is in an email to slice the domain out; explains the -1 sentinel value clearly with a code example of the not-found check

questions.prompt: Build a Chowdeck order confirmation message using template literals. Declare: const customerName = "Amaka", const restaurant = "Yellow Chilli", const item = "Egusi Soup & Eba", const price = 3200, const deliveryTime = 35. Then log a multi-line order confirmation using a backtick template literal that includes at least four line breaks and embeds all five variables. The output should look like a real order receipt — include a header, the customer name, restaurant, item, price with the ₦ symbol, and estimated delivery time.

criteria.description: All five variables are embedded in the template literal; the output spans at least four lines; the price appears with the ₦ symbol; the template literal uses actual line breaks (not \n) inside the backtick string
Uses a single backtick template literal (not multiple console.log calls); line breaks are actual newlines inside the template literal, not \n escape sequences; all five variables embedded with ${}
All five variables declared with const; the template literal looks like a real receipt (structured, readable); no syntax errors

questions.prompt: Write a URL inspector for a Nigerian news website. Declare const url = "https://techcabal.com/2025/04/nigerian-tech-news". Then log results for all four of the following with labels: (a) whether the URL is secure — use startsWith("https"); (b) whether the URL contains the word "nigeria" (case-sensitive check — it should return false since the URL has "nigerian"); (c) extract just the domain — slice from after "https://" to the first "/" after the domain. Use indexOf("/", 8) to find the first slash after "https://" (the 8 skips past the protocol); (d) split the URL by "/" and log the resulting array.

criteria.description: (a) true; (b) false — the URL has 'nigerian' not 'nigeria' exact match; (c) 'techcabal.com' — slice(8, indexOf('/', 8)); (d) an array of url parts split by '/'
startsWith used for (a); includes used for (b); slice combined with indexOf(str, startIndex) form used for (c); split("/") used for (d)
All logs have descriptive labels; expressions are readable; no unnecessary intermediate variables; no syntax errors

questions.prompt: Explain why string methods in JavaScript do not modify the original string. What does this mean for how you use them in code? Give an example showing a common mistake (expecting the original to change) and the correct way to use the method.

criteria.description: Correctly explains that strings in JavaScript are immutable — methods return a new string without changing the original; correctly identifies that the result must be stored in a variable to be used
Shows the mistake clearly — e.g. calling name.trim() but then logging name and being confused that spaces remain — and the fix: const clean = name.trim(); console.log(clean)

questions.prompt: You are processing a product CSV line from a Lagos market database. Declare const csvLine = "Tomatoes,₦1800,Fresh,Lagos,In Stock". Then: (a) split the line by "," and log the resulting array; (b) log the number of fields (the array's length — log csvLine.split(",").length); (c) log whether the line includes the word "Fresh" using includes(); (d) replace "In Stock" with "✓ Available" using replace() and log the new string; (e) log the last 8 characters of the original csvLine using slice(-8).

criteria.description: (a) array of 5 elements: ["Tomatoes", "₦1800", "Fresh", "Lagos", "In Stock"]; (b) 5; (c) true; (d) "Tomatoes,₦1800,Fresh,Lagos,✓ Available"; (e) "In Stock" — last 8 characters
split(",") used for (a) and (b); includes() for (c); replace() for (d); slice(-8) with negative index for (e)
All logs have descriptive labels; template literals or clear console.log calls used; no var; no syntax errors

questions.prompt: Declare const password = "MyP@ssw0rd!". Write a password strength checker that logs all four of the following with labels: (a) the password length using .length; (b) whether the password is at least 8 characters — log a boolean; (c) whether the password contains "@" using includes(); (d) log the password with all vowels replaced — use replaceAll() to replace each vowel (a, e, i, o, u — lowercase only) with "*", chaining four replaceAll() calls. Log the censored version.

criteria.description: (a) 11; (b) true (11 >= 8); (c) true; (d) "MyP@ssw0rd!" → "MyP@ssw0rd!" — lowercase vowels replaced: no lowercase vowels exist in this string, so result = "MyP@ssw0rd!" (full marks if student correctly chains four replaceAll calls even if no vowels are present in the test string)
.length property (not method) used for (a); comparison with >= for (b); includes() for (c); four replaceAll() calls chained for (d) — one for each vowel a, e, i, o, u
All four logs have descriptive labels; replaceAll() calls are chained in a single expression; no var; no syntax errors

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between `replace()` and `replaceAll()` in JavaScript. Give a real-world example where using `replace()` instead of `replaceAll()` would produce the wrong result. Then explain when you would deliberately use `replace()` over `replaceAll()`.

criteria.description: Correctly explains that replace() only replaces the first occurrence while replaceAll() replaces every occurrence; the real-world bug example is valid — e.g. stripping all dashes from a phone number where replace("-", "") leaves all but the first dash

The deliberate use case for replace() is valid and specific — e.g. replacing only the first occurrence of a keyword in a template, correcting only the first error in a string while preserving later occurrences intentionally

questions.prompt: Build a user display name formatter. Declare: let rawInput = "  adeola ADEYEMI-JOHNSON  ". Then: (a) clean the input — trim whitespace and convert to lowercase, store in a const called cleanInput; (b) log whether cleanInput starts with "adeola"; (c) log the position of "-" in cleanInput using indexOf; (d) extract and log the part before the hyphen using slice(0, indexOf("-")); (e) log the number of characters in cleanInput using .length.

criteria.description: cleanInput = "adeola adeyemi-johnson"; (b) true; (c) 14 — position of "-"; (d) "adeola adeyemi" — slice from 0 to 14; (e) 22 — length of cleanInput
trim().toLowerCase() chained for (a); startsWith() for (b); indexOf("-") for (c); slice(0, cleanInput.indexOf("-")) for (d) — combining indexOf with slice; .length property for (e)
rawInput declared with let as specified; cleanInput declared with const; all logs have labels; methods correctly applied to cleanInput (not rawInput) for checks (b)–(e); no syntax errors

questions.prompt: You receive a Flutterwave transaction reference string: const ref = "FLW-LIVE-NGOZI-20250401-4821". Log all five of the following with labels: (a) split the reference by "-" and log the resulting array; (b) log whether the reference includes "LIVE" using includes(); (c) log whether the reference ends with a 4-digit number — check endsWith("4821"); (d) extract the date portion "20250401" using slice — find the right positions by inspecting the string; (e) create a display-friendly version: replace "FLW-LIVE" with "TXN" using replace() and log it.

criteria.description: (a) ["FLW", "LIVE", "NGOZI", "20250401", "4821"]; (b) true; (c) true; (d) "20250401" — slice(9, 17) or equivalent correct positions; (e) "TXN-NGOZI-20250401-4821"
split("-") for (a); includes() for (b); endsWith() for (c); slice with correct positions for (d); replace() for (e); all results logged with descriptive labels
All logs are clearly labeled; no var; expressions are readable; correct method used for each task; no syntax errors

questions.prompt: Explain how `.slice()` works in JavaScript strings. What does the second argument control? What happens when you omit the second argument? What does a negative index mean? Give a concrete example using a Nigerian name or data string that demonstrates all three behaviours.

criteria.description: Correctly explains that slice(start, end) extracts from start up to but not including end; omitting end extracts from start to the end of the string; negative index counts from the end of the string (-1 = last char, -3 = last 3 chars)
All three behaviours are shown using the same concrete string — e.g. an email address or phone number — with clear annotations showing which characters each slice returns
